paragraphs.body: The Image Essay In the article, “Miffy, The Dutch Rabbit Turned Asian Hero,” Rosa Jisoo Pyo observes the Asian love for Miffy and defines the idea of cuteness as timeless and lifelong in Asia. Pyo explores the process of how Miffy is created with simple lines and four main colors. She opens her article by telling us that while Miffy might be new to the West, she has been a long-time friend in Asia. Pyo supports her point that Miffy isn’t just for children by developing several important ideas. She notes that childhood doesn’t have to end and can continue into adulthood, she illustrates this by naming big brands like Mulberry and Tommy Hilfiger that have made collaborations with Miffy for consumers of all ages. She also verifies her point by sharing testimonies from Asian women and how Miffy helped them embrace their childhood. Shannen Young, who saw herself in Miffy as a child, rediscovered her connection in adulthood. Maggie Zhou reflects on being bullied for liking anime and kpop before these interests became embraced by the West. Lastly, Fabiha Yeaqub who grew up too fast and toughened too soon, heals her inner child by reclaiming her soft and gentle qualities with Miffy.

annotations.text: Fabiha Yeaqub who grew up too fast and toughened too soon, heals her inner child by reclaiming her soft and gentle qualities with Miffy.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! This feels like an intentional "pointing" move (the "art" of summary)

paragraphs.body: Many girls have gone through the “Pink-Hating” phase in which anything remotely pink, cute, or fuzzy was deemed too girly and therefore shameful, lame, weak, and shallow– nothing any girl wanted to be viewed as or associated with. Smart, strong, and well-liked girls weren’t obsessed or self-absorbed in their appearance, but instead were cool girls that liked sports, playing video games, and were considered “one of the boys.” Being a girl was not cool because anything the opposite of a man and masculinity was considered uncool.

annotations.text: Being a girl was not cool because anything the opposite of a man and masculinity was considered uncool.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! But where did these ideas come from? What do you mean?

paragraphs.body: When I was ten years old, I was still obsessed with pink, loved bows and ribbons with dresses, and enjoyed playing with dolls. But three years later I had caught “Pink-hating” symptoms, where I cut my hair shorter, wore all black, and hated makeup. I became deathly allergic to pink, but the funny thing is that nothing about me changed. I still liked action games and TV shows, I still liked the color pink and bows, and I still hated playing sports. My likes and dislikes hadn’t changed, regardless of this new-found personality. I didn’t become any smarter than I already was. I wasn’t more confident than before. And I certainly wasn’t cooler than before because I simply was cosplaying a fantasy. I found myself watching things because other people around me were.

annotations.text: found myself watching things because other people around me were.
But three years later I had caught “Pink-hating” symptoms, where I cut my hair shorter, wore all black, and hated makeup.
annotations.comment: Interesting. So you're showing the above ideas without telling them. This paragraph, because it's so concrete and specific, is more powerful. Do you really need the paragraph above?

paragraphs.body: Pyo touches on the topic of Asian centered interests and hobbies when she notes Zhou’s experiences: “For Zhou, she remembers being bullied for watching anime and listening to K-pop before they were embraced by the West.” (Pyo) Pyo points out the hypocrisy of interests and hobbies that only become popular in the West when accepted by white people. This has resonated with me in my own experiences while growing up. I grew up in the Middle East in Asia, where Tv channels had a mix of cartoons, anime, and sitcoms. When others were watching iCarly and Victorious, I was watching Naruto and One Piece, which were the most popular shows at the time on our TV channels back in my country among children alike. These shows later became some of the most popular anime, gaining a large fan base. Even when I had indulged in these shows, I was always faced with doubt and uncertainty by male classmates and friends. As if these activities only catered towards men, and girls couldn’t possibly enjoy them while simultaneously being feminine. As if women are not three-dimensional beings with thoughts and depth, with personal interests and hobbies that don't center around male fantasies.

annotations.text: Pyo touches on the topic of Asian centered interests and hobbies when she notes Zhou’s experiences: “For Zhou, she remembers being bullied for watching anime and listening to K-pop before they were embraced by the West.” (Pyo) Pyo points out the hypocrisy of interests and hobbies that only become popular in the West when accepted by white people. This has resonated with me in my own experiences while growing up.
annotations.comment: Interesting ideas, here, but are they in the "right" order?

paragraphs.body: A lot of men don’t really understand the appeal of collecting plushies or character stuffed toys, like Miffy. But for many Asian immigrants and Asian-Americans Miffy is much more than just a cute plush, but rather a symbol of resilience, strength, and growth. To stay tender in face of adversity is the definition of strength. Pyo argues this when she interviews Yeaqub, “Miffy is my way of reclaiming myself and just be okay. That it's okay to be soft. That it's okay to be gentle.” (Pyo) In making this comment, Pyo emphasizes the importance of healing your inner child and revisiting that brings you joy, even when it makes you softer and gentler. A sentiment that a lot of women are shamed for having. “You are weak because you are soft.” I have heard this sentence from so many people in my life, yet with no proof. I found myself the strongest when I was gentle with myself, soft in tone, and patient with my inner child. When I was younger, I always felt this sense of loss of belonging like a random piece of puzzle, but this isn’t the case anymore. Rather than finding fault in my femininity, I have found strength in it. No longer being tied to a single definition of who I am or could be. Instead, I embrace a life full of cute and fuzzy things like Miffy, finding bridges in those differences and creating meaningful connections through them.

annotations.text: To stay tender in face of adversity is the definition of strength.

paragraphs.body: Humans are complex creatures, and no single word could possibly define the complexity of a single person's whole persona and life. But we assume this to be the case for women. We tell women what they are, then get angry when they comply. Tell them what to like, and then turn around to shame them for liking them. Like a paradox, with no real solution.

annotations.text: women what they are, then get angry when they comply.
annotations.comment: Who is "we"? Also, what do you mean by tell them what to do then get angry when they comply? Lots to talk about!!
